critique.summary: The article describes Horizon, a GPU-accelerated terminal application written in Rust that offers an infinite canvas for managing multiple terminal sessions. It highlights features such as pan and zoom navigation, workspace grouping, full terminal emulation, AI agent integration, git status panels, and session persistence.
mainPoints: Horizon provides an infinite 2D canvas to organize terminal sessions as panels that can be placed, resized, and grouped freely.
It supports multiple workspaces with color-coding and various layout modes like rows, columns, grid, stack, and cascade.
The terminal emulation is powered by the Alacritty engine, supporting 24-bit color, mouse reporting, scrollback, and keyboard protocols.
Horizon integrates AI agents such as Claude Code and Codex with session persistence and usage dashboards.
Built-in git integration allows monitoring repository changes with inline diffs and hunk-level details.
Smart detection enables clickable URLs and file paths directly from terminal output.
A live settings editor with YAML syntax highlighting allows instant configuration changes.
Sessions persist across restarts, restoring workspace and terminal states.
The project is open source, built with Rust 2024 edition, and uses GPU rendering frameworks like wgpu.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article appear accurate based on the publicly available GitHub repository for Horizon. The described features correspond with the README and documentation provided in the repository, which is actively maintained and includes detailed descriptions of functionality, installation instructions, and dependencies. The use of known technologies such as Rust, Alacritty terminal engine, and wgpu for GPU acceleration is consistent with the claims. There are no obvious factual errors or misleading statements.
considerations: The article does not mention any performance benchmarks or comparisons with other terminal emulators to quantify the benefits of GPU acceleration or infinite canvas.
Security considerations related to running AI agents and terminal sessions are not discussed.
The level of community adoption, user feedback, or long-term maintenance plans are not addressed.
Potential limitations or system requirements beyond Rust 1.85+ and Linux GPU headers are not detailed.
The article does not provide external sources or reviews to corroborate the utility or stability of Horizon.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around Horizon, a GPU-accelerated infinite-canvas terminal, with users sharing experiences, feature requests, and comparisons to similar tools. Comments highlight usability improvements, technical challenges, and potential inspirations from other interfaces.

comments.author: tekacs
comments.text:
This is fun! I switched to https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux for a while, but had to switch back to Ghostty due to its unreliability, high memory and CPU usage and a bunch of bugs.

This makes a lot of sense, but... it'd be great to allow pulling out of a canvas into a second canvas for those of us with multiple screens (you at least end up needing one window per screen).

In general it feels like... more structure rather than less feels like it'd be the smoothest experience. I'll play with your Ctrl+K shortcut and see if it ends up feeling like I can get everywhere that I need quickly.

But... nice work!

Note for jj users like me: you need to `git lfs pull` if you want to `cargo run --release`!

Update: No luck creating any 'shell' workspaces (it looks like you use GNU-only flags to script) – I'll push a fix once I find it.

Also: the AGENTS.md is wrong JFYI - it points to portable-pty, when this is using alacritty_terminal's tty (on rustix-openpty)
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed, experience-based feedback including technical issues and suggestions, making it highly relevant and credible.

comments.author: ms_menardi
comments.text:
This is great! Two things I noticed immediately:

the settings window overlaps with the minimap.

I think it'd be neat if you could zoom? The reason I'd use this over a tiling window manager is to fit more terminals on a screen at once. I think if there was a capability like the Strategic Zoom in Supreme Commander (video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9hJY7exr9KU) it would be much easier to manage many terminals at once, and take advantage of the infinite canvas.
comments.analysis: Offers practical UI improvement ideas grounded in user experience and external examples, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: cadamsdotcom
comments.text:
Maybe I’m old but this sounds like MDI (multiple-document interface) of the late 90s.

Went the way of the dodo probably because it’s hard for non power users to grok.

But this is a power user tool - seeems like a fit!
comments.analysis: Provides historical context and user perspective that helps frame Horizon's niche, adding depth to the conversation.

comments.author: cedws
comments.text:
Spatial memory is really underutilised in computing.

When libghostty[0] releases maybe you could use that so you don't have to build everything from scratch.

[0]: https://mitchellh.com/writing/libghostty-is-coming
comments.analysis: Suggests leveraging existing tools to improve development efficiency, a practical and relevant insight.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.text:
Feels very Niri like, in a positive way. Niri's "scrollable" interface always has room to the right & down!

One of the Niri tools I built for myself is to let me insert a new row up top, to expand up. Thats afaik not super easy in base Niri, constraints the spatial growth some.

I'm pretty close to having some persistence of terminal windows, tmux, etc in Niri, with some tools to dump current state nicely. Doing an actual restore operation would be a good thing to build towards.
comments.analysis: Draws parallels to related projects and suggests future enhancements, showing informed engagement.

comments.author: fritzo
comments.text: I was hoping this would be an infinite length terminal view, like the opening backstory in Star Wars
comments.analysis: Expresses a personal expectation that is somewhat metaphorical, less technical but reflects user anticipation.

comments.author: sakopov
comments.text: Will there be any built releases available?
comments.analysis: A straightforward inquiry about release availability, useful but minimal in content.

comments.author: hokkos
comments.text: The last thing I want is the figma experience for my terminal
comments.analysis: A brief, subjective comment expressing dislike, lacking elaboration or constructive input.
